nuggets.headline: Radiohead's *OK Computer* gave an "apocalyptic warning" a decade before *In Rainbows*.
nuggets.text: Radiohead has a decades-long history of exploring the creeping dehumanization caused by the electronic world. Their 1997 masterpiece, *OK Computer*, was already described as an "apocalyptic warning" about this digital intrusion, a thematic through-line that defines their quest for more from music and creative experience.

source.title: Radiohead, In Rainbows. and how giving away a near perfect… | by Stephen Blackford | Medium
source.quoteSnippet: 'decades long dire warnings of the imposition and creeping dehumanisation the electronic world of machines would cause', 'apocalyptic warning about the electronic world'

nuggets.imageCaption: Radiohead's *OK Computer* warned of electronic dehumanization.

nuggets.headline: Ed O’Brien's first arpeggio establishes "Weird Fishes" core harmony.
nuggets.text: The song's steady drum groove never signals section changes, but its momentum comes from the arrangement's increasing density. Ed O’Brien plays the initial guitar arpeggio, laying out the Em7 and F#m7 harmony, with Thom Yorke adding a complementary inversion and Jonny Greenwood layering a third arpeggio later, creating a shimmering effect reminiscent of minimalist composers like Steve Reich.

source.title: A Harmonic Analysis of Radiohead’s “Weird Fishes/Arpeggi” – Flypaper
source.quoteSnippet: The track starts with a solid drum groove that stays the same throughout most of the song. The drums do not signal the subdivision of the arrangement in sections, which is unusual for pop music. Radiohead creates momentum in 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' texturally, by increasing the density of the arrangement with added layers. The first guitar arpeggio is played by Ed O’Brien. The first guitar lays out the harmony of the first part of the song. It plays two shell voicings (root, third, and seventh) of the chords Em7 and F#m7. The harmony then develops into A and Gmaj7, also in shell voicing form. On the fourth chord (Gmaj7), a second guitar superimposes another arpeggio. In live versions, Thom Yorke plays this second guitar part. A third arpeggio, played by Jonny Greenwood, is added to the texture at 1:48. This layering method with similar timbres is reminiscent of minimalist works by Steve Reich and Terry Riley.
source.locator: Sections on arrangement and guitar parts

nuggets.imageCaption: Transcribed arpeggio layering from Radiohead's "Weird Fishes."

nuggets.headline: Philip Selway's solo work shares Radiohead's nuanced rhythmic depth.
nuggets.text: If you're drawn to the intricate, evolving textures and rhythmic constructions in "Weird Fishes/Arpeggi," explore Philip Selway's solo albums. Radiohead's own drummer, Selway's individual music often features a similar refined approach, combining delicate orchestrations with a thoughtful rhythmic foundation that resonates with the band's characteristic sound.

source.title: MTO 19.1: Hesselink, Radiohead's “Pyramid Song”
source.quoteSnippet: Radiohead is known for metrical shifts and interesting rhythmic constructions
source.locator: Various mentions of Radiohead's rhythmic qualities

nuggets.imageCaption: Philip Selway, Radiohead drummer, explores refined solo sound.

artistSummary: Radiohead, formed in Abingdon, Oxfordshire, is an English rock band celebrated for its artistic integrity and experimental sound. They consistently explore the impact of technology on society, often warning against the dehumanizing effects of the electronic world.
